question_parts.task_instruction: Explain how an increase in consumer income affects the demand for a normal good, using a supply-and-demand diagram where appropriate.

criteria.name: Demand shift identification
criteria.description: Student correctly identifies that an increase in income shifts the demand curve to the right for a normal good.
partial_credit_rules: 1 mark for identifying shift without explaining direction
1 mark for identifying normal good without linking to shift
common_misconceptions: confusing normal and inferior goods
stating price changes rather than demand shifts

criteria.name: Price and quantity outcome
criteria.description: Student correctly explains that equilibrium price and quantity both rise as a result of the demand shift.
partial_credit_rules: 1 mark for correctly identifying price or quantity change alone
common_misconceptions: stating only price rises without quantity
confusing movement along a curve with a shift
no_credit_conditions: both price and quantity stated as falling

criteria.name: Diagram quality
criteria.description: Student describes or draws a correctly labelled supply-and-demand diagram showing the rightward shift of demand and new equilibrium.
required_evidence: reference to diagram with labelled axes or curves
common_misconceptions: shifting supply instead of demand
unlabelled axes
no_credit_conditions: no diagram or description of diagram

bucket_definitions.label: Excellent
bucket_definitions.description: All three criteria fully met with clear explanation and correct diagram.

bucket_definitions.label: Good
bucket_definitions.description: Two criteria fully met; one minor gap.

bucket_definitions.label: Adequate
bucket_definitions.description: Core demand shift identified with partial evidence for outcomes.

bucket_definitions.label: Poor
bucket_definitions.description: Some relevant content but significant gaps or one misconception.

bucket_definitions.label: Very Poor
bucket_definitions.description: Minimal relevant content; shows awareness of topic only.

bucket_definitions.label: Fail
bucket_definitions.description: No relevant content or completely incorrect.

question_parts.task_instruction: Using the concept of price elasticity of demand, explain why a 10% rise in price may lead to a larger or smaller percentage fall in quantity demanded depending on the good.

criteria.name: PED definition
criteria.description: Student correctly defines price elasticity of demand as the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in price.
partial_credit_rules: 1 mark for stating PED measures responsiveness of demand to price without formula
common_misconceptions: using absolute changes rather than percentage changes
inverting numerator and denominator
no_credit_conditions: no reference to percentage or proportional changes

criteria.name: Elastic vs inelastic distinction
criteria.description: Student correctly distinguishes elastic demand (PED > 1, larger % fall in QD) from inelastic demand (PED < 1, smaller % fall in QD) with appropriate examples.
required_evidence: PED > 1 leads to larger fall in QD
PED < 1 leads to smaller fall in QD
at least one appropriate example (luxury vs necessity, or substitute availability)
partial_credit_rules: 1 mark for correct statement about elastic or inelastic alone
2 marks for both elastic and inelastic without examples
common_misconceptions: stating elastic means price-sensitive without quantifying
confusing PED with PES

bucket_definitions.label: Excellent
bucket_definitions.description: Full PED definition, correct elastic/inelastic distinction, appropriate examples.

bucket_definitions.label: Good
bucket_definitions.description: Mostly correct with one minor omission.

bucket_definitions.label: Adequate
bucket_definitions.description: PED concept understood; elastic/inelastic partially addressed.

bucket_definitions.label: Poor
bucket_definitions.description: Partial definition; elastic/inelastic confused or missing.

bucket_definitions.label: Very Poor
bucket_definitions.description: Minimal relevant content about price and demand.

bucket_definitions.label: Fail
bucket_definitions.description: No relevant content.

question_parts.task_instruction: Explain the concept of market failure due to negative externalities. Illustrate with an example and discuss one government policy to address it.

criteria.name: Market failure definition
criteria.description: Student correctly defines market failure as a situation where the free market does not allocate resources efficiently.
required_evidence: free market or price mechanism failure
partial_credit_rules: 1 mark for identifying market failure without defining inefficiency
common_misconceptions: equating market failure with market collapse
defining as government failure

criteria.name: Negative externality explanation
criteria.description: Student correctly explains negative externalities as third-party costs not reflected in the market price, leading to overproduction.
partial_credit_rules: 2 marks for third-party cost plus overproduction without MSC/MPC language
common_misconceptions: stating externality only affects the producer
confusing negative externality with negative income effect

criteria.name: Example
criteria.description: Student provides a relevant, correctly applied example of a negative externality (e.g. pollution, smoking, congestion).
required_evidence: link between example and third-party cost
partial_credit_rules: 1 mark for named example without explanation
common_misconceptions: using a positive externality example

criteria.name: Government policy
criteria.description: Student correctly explains one government policy (e.g. Pigouvian tax, regulation, cap-and-trade) and how it addresses the externality.
required_evidence: mechanism by which policy internalises the externality
partial_credit_rules: 1 mark for naming a policy without explanation
2 marks for mechanism without explicit link to externality correction
common_misconceptions: describing subsidies as a policy for negative externalities
stating ban is the only option

bucket_definitions.label: Excellent
bucket_definitions.description: All four criteria fully met; clear, accurate, and well-illustrated.

bucket_definitions.label: Good
bucket_definitions.description: Three criteria fully met; one minor gap in evidence or policy explanation.

bucket_definitions.label: Adequate
bucket_definitions.description: Core externality concept present; policy or example partially addressed.

bucket_definitions.label: Poor
bucket_definitions.description: Partial understanding; one or two criteria barely met.

bucket_definitions.label: Very Poor
bucket_definitions.description: Minimal relevant content; some awareness of externalities but largely incorrect.

bucket_definitions.label: Fail
bucket_definitions.description: No relevant content or completely incorrect.

question_parts.task_instruction: Compare and contrast monopoly and perfect competition in terms of price, output, and efficiency. Include a discussion of deadweight loss.

criteria.name: Perfect competition characteristics
criteria.description: Student correctly identifies key characteristics of perfect competition: many firms, price-taking, P = MC, allocative efficiency.
partial_credit_rules: 1 mark for listing features without efficiency implications

criteria.name: Monopoly characteristics
criteria.description: Student correctly identifies monopoly as a single seller with market power that charges P > MC and produces below the competitive output.
partial_credit_rules: 1 mark for identifying single seller alone
2 marks for P > MC without output comparison
common_misconceptions: stating monopolist always earns profit in short run
confusing monopsony with monopoly

criteria.name: Deadweight loss
criteria.description: Student correctly explains deadweight loss as the welfare loss arising from monopoly underproduction relative to the competitive equilibrium.
required_evidence: triangle in diagram or equivalent description
partial_credit_rules: 1 mark for naming deadweight loss without explanation
2 marks for correct concept without diagram or triangle description
common_misconceptions: stating deadweight loss is producer profit
ignoring consumer surplus reduction

criteria.name: Comparison and synthesis
criteria.description: Student explicitly compares the two structures in terms of at least two dimensions (price, output, efficiency, consumer welfare) and draws a clear conclusion.
required_evidence: explicit comparison on at least two dimensions
conclusion about relative efficiency or consumer welfare
partial_credit_rules: 1 mark for comparison on one dimension only

bucket_definitions.label: Excellent
bucket_definitions.description: Comprehensive comparison; deadweight loss correctly explained; clear synthesis.

bucket_definitions.label: Good
bucket_definitions.description: Good comparison; one minor gap in deadweight loss or efficiency discussion.

bucket_definitions.label: Adequate
bucket_definitions.description: Both structures described; comparison partial; deadweight loss mentioned.

bucket_definitions.label: Poor
bucket_definitions.description: One structure well described; comparison weak; deadweight loss vague.

bucket_definitions.label: Very Poor
bucket_definitions.description: Minimal relevant content; one structure barely described.

bucket_definitions.label: Fail
bucket_definitions.description: No relevant content.
